summary: Gruber examines a Wall Street Journal report that Procter & Gamble collaborated with a Chinese trade group backed by the state to develop CAID, a device fingerprinting technique designed to circumvent Apple's upcoming App Tracking Transparency privacy rules. He notes that while Apple can restrict access to IDFA, clever developers can combine other accessible data points like IP addresses into tracking identifiers. The core tension is whether Apple will enforce its rules against apps backed by the Chinese government, particularly WeChat. Gruber reads Apple's official statement as adamant but questions whether Tencent believes it can act with impunity because Apple wouldn't dare pull WeChat from China.
key_points: Procter & Gamble, a major American company, partnered with a Chinese state-backed advertising association to develop CAID fingerprinting that circumvents Apple's privacy rules
Apple faces a whack-a-mole problem: blocking IDFA access doesn't prevent developers from combining other accessible signals like IP addresses into tracking identifiers
The real test is enforcement in China — whether Apple would actually pull WeChat or other major apps backed by the PRC government
IDFA was Apple's attempt to work cooperatively with advertisers on a sanctioned tracking identifier that respected privacy, but the industry's response proved they have no respect for user control
Apple's public statement applies rules 'equally to all developers around the world, including Apple' — but enforcement is the question
money_quotes: Not a good look for a major American company like Procter & Gamble to be in cahoots with a Chinese trade group to circumvent Apple's new privacy rules.

The whack-a-mole aspect of Apple's new privacy rules is that while Apple can restrict access to the API that provides access to the IDFA identifier, clever developers can find (perhaps infinite) other ways to combine things they do have access to into a unique, or even just 'close enough to unique to be useful for tracking', identifier.

What is Apple going to do if WeChat is flagged for circumventing the App Store privacy rules, and Tencent says 'No thank you' to Apple's rules, that they're going to do it anyway because they have the backing of the PRC?
I don't read diplomat-ese fluently, but that statement seems adamant: 'all developers around the world, even Apple'.
I wonder, though, if Tencent believes they can track users with impunity because Apple wouldn't dare pull WeChat from the Chinese App Store.
These companies have no respect for user privacy or user control, even with IDFA.
That'll only work if Apple backs up its rules with enforcement — even in China.
key_insight: Apple's privacy rules are only as strong as its willingness to enforce them against powerful actors like the Chinese government, and the CAID scheme will be the ultimate test of whether Apple's privacy stance is principled or selective.
